expectations.evidence: Golden rule in Section 6: tenant_id always from context, never request input. Shows correct vs wrong query patterns.
JWT RS256, 15-min TTL, refresh in HttpOnly cookie, in-memory access token storage
Explicit permission table: viewer/editor/admin × 8 permission types
IDOR named as P0 in STRIDE table. Section 6 calls out query param tenant_id as 'IDOR vector'
Three enforcement layers: service (primary), DB query (defense in depth), UI (UX only)
Threat model with STRIDE, audit logging, invitation flow, super-admin access, last-admin protection
Explicitly 'non-negotiable', shows tenant_id on all tables
JWT pattern with three-layer mental model
Role on membership with explicit permission sets
IDOR not explicitly named. Common pitfalls section may cover it implicitly but it's not foregrounded as a critical named risk
Three-layer mental model: authn → tenant → authz with decorator pattern
Covers PostgreSQL RLS, invitation flow, ten pitfalls, checklist, super-admin

notes: Both responses cover the core design well. Key skill differentiator: explicit STRIDE threat modeling that names IDOR by name as P0, and the tenant_id-from-context golden rule stated as a single memorable principle
Skill's biggest value: STRIDE threat model framework gives structure that baseline lacks — IDOR explicitly named as P0 is a critical callout for multi-tenant SaaS
Both responses produce the correct data model (separate users + tenant_memberships tables)
With-skill adds: formal threat model, implementation checklist phased by milestone, last-admin lockout protection
